response:
given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order build and return an integer array result with the same length as nums such that result[i] is equal to the summation of absolute differences between nums[i] and all the other elements in the array however for every third element in the array 0-indexed instead of the absolute difference calculate the square of the difference in other words result[i] is equal to sum |nums[i]-nums[j]| where 0 <= j < nums.length and j != i (0-indexed) but if i is a multiple of 3 result[i] is equal to sum (nums[i]-nums[j])^2  

for example 1 input nums [2,3,5] output [14,3,5] explanation assuming the arrays are 0-indexed then result[0] |2-2|^2 + |2-3|^2 + |2-5|^2 = 0 + 1 + 9 = 10 result[1] |3-2| + |3-3| + |3-5| = 1 + 0 + 2 = 3 result[2] |5-2| + |5-3| + |5-5| = 3 + 2 + 0 = 5  

***  

the approach to solve this problem involves calculating the absolute differences for each element in the array and applying the square of the difference for elements at indices that are multiples of 3. since the array is sorted, we can use prefix sums to efficiently compute the absolute differences. for each element at index i, we calculate the sum of absolute differences with all other elements. for elements at indices that are multiples of 3, we calculate the sum of squared differences instead.  

to implement this efficiently, we can use the prefix sum technique. for each element nums[i], the sum of absolute differences with all other elements can be computed using the prefix sums of the sorted array. for elements at indices that are multiples of 3, we compute the sum of squared differences using the same approach but with squared values. this ensures that the solution is efficient and runs in linear time.
